answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on the elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any injury or trauma to the elbow before the pain started?
answers.intake_a3: no, but I was playing tennis when the pain started
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Can you describe what activities or movements make the elbow pain worse or better?
answers.intake_a5: tennis makes it worse and rest makes it better
answers.intake_q6: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your hand or arm?
answers.intake_q7: Any fever, chills, or night sweats since the elbow pain started?
answers.intake_q8: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your elbow pain so far?
answers.intake_a8: I iced it the first day
answers.intake_q9: Any difficulty gripping or lifting objects with your affected arm?
answers.intake_q10: Any prior history of elbow problems or repetitive strain injuries?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began while playing tennis without any preceding injury or trauma. She reports that tennis exacerbates the pain, while rest alleviates it. There is no associated swelling, redness, or warmth. She denies numbness, tingling, weakness, fever, chills, or night sweats. She has experienced pain with lifting objects but no prior history of elbow problems or repetitive strain injuries. She initially tried icing the elbow on the first day of symptoms. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain worsened by activity and relieved by rest, without signs of infection or neurological involvement. 

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; avoid exceeding recommended doses. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if there is worsening pain, development of swelling, redness, warmth, fever, numbness, tingling, or weakness in the arm or hand. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time; consider imaging or specialist referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-8 weeks or worsen despite conservative management.

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…
